usage_notes: Can be physical or digital. Often used in expressions like 'un livre de poche' (paperback).
common_collocations: lire un livre, ouvrir un livre, fermer un livre
image_context: An open colorful book resting on a wooden table in a library
example_2: Le livre est sur la table.
example_2_translation: The book is on the table.
example_3: Elle a acheté un nouveau livre hier.
example_3_translation: She bought a new book yesterday.
pronunciation_tips: The 'livre' rhymes with 'vivre'. The final 'e' is silent.
memory_hook: A book 'livre' you to other worlds
common_mistakes: Don't confuse 'livre' (book) with 'livrer' (to deliver)

usage_notes: Refers to residential buildings. 'À la maison' means 'at home'.
common_collocations: ma maison, grande maison, petite maison
image_context: A cozy French house with blue shutters and a garden
example_3: Nous habitons dans une petite maison.
example_3_translation: We live in a small house.
pronunciation_tips: The 'ai' sounds like 'ɛ'. The final 'n' is nasal, not pronounced separately.
memory_hook: A house 'maison' is your 'maze-on' to explore
common_mistakes: Don't use 'maison' for apartments; use 'appartement' instead.

primary_meaning: to put food in your mouth and chew
usage_notes: Regular -er verb. Note spelling change: je mange, nous mangeons.
common_collocations: bien manger, manger une pomme, manger à midi
image_context: A person eating a meal at a dining table with fresh produce
example_3: Il mange du pain tous les jours.
pronunciation_tips: The 'ge' becomes 'j' sound. The 'an' is nasal (like 'on' in song).
common_mistakes: Remember: je mange (not 'mange'), nous mangeons (not 'mangons')

usage_notes: Irregular verb: je bois, tu bois, il boit, nous buvons, vous buvez, ils boivent.
common_collocations: boire de l'eau, boire un café, boire du vin
image_context: A glass of water being held by hands showing refreshment
example_2: Il boit du café le matin.
example_2_translation: He drinks coffee in the morning.
pronunciation_tips: The 'oi' sounds like 'wa'. The 'r' is guttural at the end.
memory_hook: To 'boire' sounds like 'why'—you ask 'why' when thirsty
common_mistakes: Irregular verb! Don't say 'je boirai' for present tense.

usage_notes: Common pet. Also used in expressions like 'avoir un chat dans la gorge' (have a frog in one's throat).
common_collocations: un chat noir, mon chat, le chat dort
image_context: A black cat sleeping curled up on a couch cushion
example_1: Le chat dort sur le canapé.
example_1_translation: The cat is sleeping on the couch.
example_3_translation: The cat was meowing at night.
pronunciation_tips: The 'ch' sounds like 'sh'. The final 't' is silent.
common_mistakes: Don't pronounce 'ch' as in English 'church'; it's 'sh'.

usage_notes: Common pet. Plural: chiens. Often appears in expressions like 'être chien' (mean).
common_collocations: mon chien, un petit chien, le chien aboie
image_context: A happy golden retriever running in a green garden
example_2: Le chien court dans le jardin.
example_2_translation: The dog runs in the garden.
pronunciation_tips: The 'ch' is 'sh'. The 'ie' is like 'yeh'. Final 'n' is nasal.
memory_hook: A 'chien' (dog) goes 'woof' to 'chien' (chain) you to play
common_mistakes: Don't confuse with 'Chine' (China). Keep the nasal 'n'.

usage_notes: Uncountable noun. Use 'un verre d'eau' (a glass of water) for countable reference.
common_collocations: boire l'eau, l'eau froide, l'eau chaude
image_context: A clear glass filled with fresh cold water with ice cubes
example_1_translation: I want a glass of water.
example_3: L'eau est essentielle pour la vie.
pronunciation_tips: Sounds like 'oh'. The 'eau' diphthong always sounds the same.
common_mistakes: Never say 'une eau'—use 'un verre d'eau' instead.

primary_meaning: baked dough made from flour and water
usage_notes: Classic French food. Common type: baguette. 'Avoir du pain sur la planche' = have a lot to do.
common_collocations: du pain, un pain, pain frais
image_context: A fresh golden baguette on a wooden cutting board
example_1: Je mange du pain au petit-déjeuner.
example_3: J'achète du pain à la boulangerie.
example_3_translation: I buy bread at the bakery.
pronunciation_tips: The 'ai' is pronounced 'ɛ'. The final 'n' is nasal.
common_mistakes: Don't forget the nasal 'n'. It's 'pain' not 'pai'.

usage_notes: Essential greeting. Use 'bonsoir' after 5–6 PM. Used until you say goodbye.
common_collocations: bonjour à tous, bonjour comment allez-vous
image_search_query: two people shaking hands smiling warmly
image_context: Two people shaking hands and smiling warmly in greeting
example_3_translation: I say hello to the teacher.
pronunciation_tips: The 'on' is nasal. The 'j' is soft. The final 'r' is guttural.
related_words: au revoir, bonsoir, s'il vous plaît
common_mistakes: Use before evening only. After 5–6 PM, say 'bonsoir'.

usage_notes: Essential politeness word. 'Merci beaucoup' = thank you very much.
common_collocations: merci beaucoup, merci pour, merci de
image_context: A person smiling and holding hands in gratitude gesture
example_3: Je te remercie. Merci de m'aider.
example_3_translation: I thank you. Thank you for helping me.
pronunciation_tips: The 'e' before 'r' is open (like in 'bed'). Final 'ci' is pronounced 'si'.
memory_hook: Say 'merci' to make 'merry' the other person
related_words: s'il vous plaît, de rien, remerciement
common_mistakes: It's 'merci', not 'merci vous'. No subject needed.

usage_notes: Always 'oui' for yes. Don't say 'oui, non' together.
common_collocations: oui merci, oui bien sûr, oui je comprends
image_search_query: person nodding YES thumbs up gesture
image_context: A person nodding YES with a thumbs up gesture
example_2: Tu veux du café? Oui, merci.
example_2_translation: Do you want coffee? Yes, please.
pronunciation_tips: Sounds like 'we'. The 'ui' diphthong is always pronounced this way.
common_mistakes: Don't say 'oui, non'—choose one response.

usage_notes: Simple 'non' is enough for polite refusal. Add 'merci' for politeness.
common_collocations: non merci, non pas, non vraiment
image_search_query: person shaking head NO hand gesture
image_context: A person shaking head NO with a hand gesture
pronunciation_tips: The 'on' is nasal, like the 'on' in 'song'. No final sound.
memory_hook: Say 'non' and tone down (no, not yes)
common_mistakes: Don't forget the nasal 'on' sound.

primary_meaning: a movable barrier for entering or exiting
usage_notes: Common household item. 'À la porte' = at the door. 'Porte' also means 'carries' as a verb.
common_collocations: ouvrir la porte, fermer la porte, frapper à la porte
image_context: A tall wooden door with a brass handle
example_2: Ferme la porte, s'il te plaît.
example_3_translation: Someone is knocking on the door.
pronunciation_tips: The 'or' is like in 'for'. The final 't' is silent.
memory_hook: A 'porte' (door) is your 'port' to another room
common_mistakes: Don't confuse 'porte' (noun) with 'porter' (to carry).

primary_meaning: an opening in a wall to let in light and air
secondary_meaning: a frame or pane of glass
usage_notes: Common household feature. Often appears with 'volets' (shutters) in French houses.
common_collocations: ouvrir la fenêtre, fermer la fenêtre, par la fenêtre
image_context: A bright open window with white curtains
pronunciation_tips: The 'e' is schwa (ə). The 'ê' is open. Final 'e' is silent.
memory_hook: A 'fenêtre' (window) is where you see 'fem' (beautiful women) pass by
common_mistakes: Don't say 'le fenêtre'—it's feminine 'la fenêtre'.

primary_meaning: a male child or young man
usage_notes: Generic term for boy. 'Un garçon manqué' = tomboy. Also used for 'waiter'.
common_collocations: un petit garçon, un jeune garçon, garçon café
image_context: A young boy smiling in a park setting
example_1: Le garçon joue dans le parc.
example_1_translation: The boy is playing in the park.
pronunciation_tips: The 'ar' is like in 'car'. The 'on' is nasal. Final 'n' silent.
common_mistakes: Don't confuse with 'garçonne' (tomboy). Keep the nasal ending.

primary_meaning: a female child or young woman
usage_notes: Generic term for girl. Can also mean 'daughter' in family context. 'Fille publique' (old term) = prostitute.
common_collocations: une petite fille, une jeune fille, ma fille
image_search_query: young girl reading book gentle smile
image_context: A young girl reading a book with a gentle smile
example_1_translation: The girl is reading a book.
pronunciation_tips: The 'i' is like 'ee'. The 'lle' is silent (just like 'y' in English 'yes').
memory_hook: A 'fille' (girl) is the 'fill' of her mother's heart
common_mistakes: Don't pronounce the 'lle'—it's just 'fee-yuh'.

usage_notes: Begins with vowel, uses 'l''. Plural: 'les hommes'. Different pronunciation in plural.
common_collocations: un homme, les hommes, homme fort
image_search_query: mature man business attire standing confidently
image_context: A mature man in business attire standing confidently
pronunciation_tips: The 'om' is nasal. Singular: 'lɔm', plural: 'lez ɔm'.
common_mistakes: Don't say 'le homme'—it's 'l'homme' (elision).

usage_notes: Can mean woman or wife depending on context. 'Ma femme' usually = my wife.
common_collocations: une femme, les femmes, femme médecin
image_context: A woman cooking in a modern kitchen
pronunciation_tips: The 'em' is nasal. The final 'e' is silent.
common_mistakes: Don't forget it's feminine 'la femme', not 'le femme'.

primary_meaning: to move from one place to another
usage_notes: Highly irregular: vais, vas, va, allons, allez, vont. Essential verb.
common_collocations: aller à, aller bien, allez-y
image_context: A person walking along a path toward a destination
pronunciation_tips: Infinitive 'aller' (ah-lay). Present: je vais (veh), nous allons (ah-loh).
common_mistakes: This is VERY irregular! Learn all forms: vais, vas, va, allons, allez, vont.

usage_notes: Irregular verb. Forms: viens, vient, venons, venez, viennent. 'Venir de' = just did.
common_collocations: venir de, venir ici, venir à
image_search_query: person walking toward camera welcoming gesture
image_context: A person walking toward the camera with welcoming gesture
pronunciation_tips: The 'enir' sounds like 'en-eer'. The 'v' is soft at the start.
common_mistakes: Don't confuse with 'tenir' (to hold). Learn: viens, vient, viennent.

primary_meaning: a motor vehicle for carrying passengers
usage_notes: French word for car. 'Auto' is also used. 'Voiture de luxe' = luxury car.
common_collocations: ma voiture, une voiture rouge, la voiture gare
image_context: A sleek red car on an open road
pronunciation_tips: The 'oi' is 'wa'. The 'tu' is soft (tyuh). Final 'e' is silent.
common_mistakes: Don't say 'char' (Quebec term)—use 'voiture' in France.

primary_meaning: an institution for teaching and learning
usage_notes: Begins with vowel: 'l'école'. 'Aller à l'école' = go to school.
common_collocations: l'école primaire, l'école secondaire, aller à l'école
image_search_query: French school building children playing yard
image_context: A French school building with children playing in the yard
pronunciation_tips: Elision with vowel: 'l'école' (lek-ɔl). The 'ol' rhymes with 'coal'.
common_mistakes: Don't say 'le école' or 'de école'—always 'l'école'.

primary_meaning: activity involving effort to accomplish a task
usage_notes: Masculine. Plural: 'travaux' (works, constructions). 'Bon travail' = good work.
common_collocations: mon travail, aller au travail, chercher du travail
image_context: A person working at a desk with papers and laptop
pronunciation_tips: The 'ai' is like 'ɛ'. The 'j' is soft like in 'measure'. Final 'l' is silent.
common_mistakes: Plural is 'travaux', not 'travails'.

usage_notes: Regular -re verb (like prendre). Forms: apprends, apprend, apprenons, apprenez.
common_collocations: apprendre une langue, apprendre à, apprendre par cœur
image_context: A student studying with books and taking notes
pronunciation_tips: The 'pp' is single sound. The 'en' is nasal. The 'dr' ends with guttural 'r'.
common_mistakes: Don't confuse with 'enseigner' (to teach). 'Apprendre' = to learn.

primary_meaning: to grasp the meaning or significance
usage_notes: Regular -re verb (like prendre). Forms: comprends, comprend, comprenons.
common_collocations: je ne comprends pas, tu comprends, comprendre une langue
image_context: A person with a thoughtful expression and raised eyebrow
pronunciation_tips: The 'com' sounds like 'kɔm'. The 'en' is nasal. The 'dr' is guttural.
common_mistakes: It's 'comprendre', not 'comprender' (Spanish spelling).

primary_meaning: a group of related persons living together
usage_notes: Feminine. 'Mon père et ma mère' (my father and mother) = my family.
common_collocations: ma famille, une grande famille, vie familiale
image_search_query: happy family four spending time outdoors
image_context: A happy family of four spending time together outdoors
pronunciation_tips: The 'ille' at the end is silent (like in 'fille'). Sounds like 'fah-mee'.
common_mistakes: Don't pronounce the 'ille'—it's 'fah-mee', not 'fah-mil'.

primary_meaning: a person with whom one has a bond of affection
usage_notes: Masculine. Begins with vowel: 'l'ami'. Feminine: 'l'amie'.
common_collocations: mon ami, meilleur ami, ami proche
image_search_query: two friends laughing talking walking together
image_context: Two friends laughing and talking while walking together
pronunciation_tips: Elision: 'l'ami' (lah-mee). The 'mi' rhymes with 'see'.
common_mistakes: Feminine is 'l'amie', masculine is 'l'ami'. Don't forget the 'e'.

primary_meaning: a public way or road in a town
usage_notes: Feminine. 'Rue de...' is common in French addresses.
common_collocations: dans la rue, rue principale, traverser la rue
image_context: A cobblestone street in a French city with shops
pronunciation_tips: The 'u' is like 'oo' in 'book'. The final 'e' is silent.
common_mistakes: It's feminine 'la rue', not 'le rue'.

primary_meaning: an establishment serving meals to customers
secondary_meaning: a place where food is prepared and served
usage_notes: Masculine. 'Au restaurant' = at the restaurant. Common in France.
common_collocations: aller au restaurant, un bon restaurant, manger au restaurant
image_context: An elegant French restaurant with tables on a sidewalk
pronunciation_tips: The 'es' is like in 'pet'. The 'or' is like in 'or'. Final 'nt' is silent.
memory_hook: 'Restaurant' (restaurant) is where you 'rest' after work and eat
common_mistakes: Don't confuse with 'café' (café/bar) or 'bistro' (casual).

primary_meaning: to obtain goods in exchange for money
usage_notes: Regular -er verb. Note accent change: j'achète, tu achètes, il achète, ils achètent.
common_collocations: acheter des fruits, acheter une maison, acheter en ligne
image_context: A person shopping at a market selecting fresh produce
pronunciation_tips: The 'ch' is soft 'sh'. The 'e' before 't' is closed (like 'ay').
common_mistakes: Watch out for accent changes in conjugation (achète, achetez).

secondary_meaning: to offer for sale or vend
usage_notes: Regular -re verb. Forms: vends, vend, vendons, vendez, vendent.
common_collocations: vendre des vêtements, vendre une maison, à vendre
image_context: A storefront with items displayed for sale
pronunciation_tips: The 'en' is nasal. The 'dr' is guttural. The 'e' before 'r' is open.
common_mistakes: Don't confuse with 'prendre' (take). It's 'vendre' (sell).

primary_meaning: the visual property of objects dependent on light
usage_notes: Feminine. Often used with 'préférée' (favorite). 'Sans couleur' = colorless.
common_collocations: couleur préférée, couleur bleue, couleur vive
image_context: Colorful paint swatches in vibrant rainbow colors
pronunciation_tips: The 'ou' is like in 'boot'. The 'l' is soft. The 'r' is guttural.
common_mistakes: Don't forget the 'u' in the middle: 'couleur', not 'coleor'.

usage_notes: Adjective. 'Grand' comes before noun. 'Une grande maison' = a big house.
common_collocations: grand bâtiment, très grand, grand et petit
image_context: A tall skyscraper reaching into blue sky
pronunciation_tips: The 'and' is nasal. The 'r' is silent at the end.
common_mistakes: Don't say 'grandement' in description. Use 'très grand'.

usage_notes: Adjective. Usually comes before noun. 'Un petit garçon' = a small boy.
common_collocations: petit jardin, petit peu, petit enfant
image_search_query: small kitten next human hand comparison
image_context: A small kitten next to a human hand for size comparison
pronunciation_tips: The 'e' is schwa (ə). The 'ti' rhymes with 'see'. Final 't' is silent.
common_mistakes: Feminine is 'petite'. 'Un petit garçon' but 'une petite fille'.

usage_notes: Irregular feminine: 'nouvelle'. Before vowel: 'nouvel'. 'Un nouvel ami' = a new friend.
common_collocations: un nouveau livre, une nouvelle voiture, quoi de nouveau
image_search_query: brand new car price tag ribbon
image_context: A brand new car with price tag and ribbon
pronunciation_tips: The 'ou' is like in 'boot'. The 'veau' rhymes with 'toe'. Final 'u' is silent.
common_mistakes: Feminine 'nouvelle', before vowel 'nouvel' (e.g., 'nouvel ami').

primary_meaning: pleasing to the eye or mind
usage_notes: Irregular feminine: 'belle'. Before vowel: 'bel'. 'Il fait beau' = nice weather.
common_collocations: beau temps, un beau tableau, belle journée
image_context: A beautiful sunset over a French landscape
pronunciation_tips: The 'eau' is like 'oh'. Before vowel: 'bel' (like 'bell'). Final 'u' is silent.
common_mistakes: Feminine 'belle', before vowel 'bel' (e.g., 'bel ami').

usage_notes: Adjective. Comes before noun. 'C'est mauvais' = it's bad. Same form for masculine/feminine singular.
common_collocations: une mauvaise idée, mauvais temps, mauvaise herbe
image_search_query: dark storm clouds gathering over city
image_context: Dark storm clouds gathering over a city
pronunciation_tips: The 'au' is like in 'law'. The 'ai' is like 'ɛ' (open). The 's' is silent.
common_mistakes: Final 's' is silent. It's 'mauvais', not 'mauvaise' (unless feminine noun).

usage_notes: Adjective. Comes before noun. 'Bon appétit!' = enjoy your meal!
common_collocations: bon restaurant, bon apétit, bonne journée
image_context: A delicious plated meal with steam rising
pronunciation_tips: The 'on' is nasal like in 'song'. The 'n' is not pronounced separately.
common_mistakes: Feminine 'bonne'. Final 'n' is nasal, not pronounced as separate 'n'.

secondary_meaning: a menu in a restaurant, or a card
usage_notes: Multiple meanings: map, menu, or card. Context determines meaning.
common_collocations: la carte postale, sur la carte, la carte du restaurant
image_context: A detailed map spread out on a table
pronunciation_tips: The 'ar' is like in 'car'. The final 'e' is silent.
common_mistakes: Don't confuse meanings: map, menu, or card based on context.

primary_meaning: a device for communicating over distance
usage_notes: Masculine. Modern version: 'téléphone portable' or 'smartphone'.
common_collocations: téléphone portable, coup de téléphone, répondre au téléphone
image_context: A modern smartphone on a wooden table
pronunciation_tips: The 'é' is closed (like 'ay'). The 'é' before 'l' is open. Final 'e' is silent.
common_mistakes: Don't say 'le phone' in formal French—use 'téléphone'.

primary_meaning: to mark letters or words on paper
usage_notes: Irregular -ire verb. Forms: écris, écrit, écrivons, écrivez, écrivent.
common_collocations: écrire une lettre, écrire un livre, écrire un email
image_context: A hand holding a pen writing on paper
pronunciation_tips: The 'é' is closed (like 'ay'). The 'cr' is 'kʁ'. The final 'e' is silent.
common_mistakes: It's irregular: 'écris', not 'écrites' for masculine singular.

primary_meaning: to look at and comprehend written text
usage_notes: Irregular -ire verb. Forms: lis, lit, lisons, lisez, lisent.
common_collocations: lire un livre, lire un journal, aimer lire
image_context: A person reading a book in a cozy armchair
pronunciation_tips: The 'i' is like 'ee'. The 'r' is guttural. The final 'e' is silent.
common_mistakes: It's irregular: 'lis' (present), 'lu' (past participle).

usage_notes: Regular -er verb. Forms: parle, parles, parlons, parlez, parlent.
common_collocations: parler français, parler à quelqu'un, parler de
image_search_query: two people engaged conversation face face
image_context: Two people engaged in conversation face to face
pronunciation_tips: The 'ar' is like in 'car'. The 'le' rhymes with 'say'.
common_mistakes: Regular -er verb. Don't add unnecessary letters.

primary_meaning: to perceive sound with the ear
secondary_meaning: to hear attentively or pay attention
usage_notes: Regular -er verb. Forms: écoute, écoutes, écoutons, écoutez, écoutent.
common_collocations: écouter de la musique, écouter quelqu'un, écouter attentivement
image_context: A person wearing headphones with eyes closed
pronunciation_tips: The 'é' is closed (like 'ay'). The 'ou' is like in 'boot'.
common_mistakes: Regular -er verb. Don't say 'écoutons' for 'je écoute'.

usage_notes: Regular -er verb. Forms: regarde, regardes, regardons, regardez, regardent.
common_collocations: regarder la télévision, regarder quelqu'un, regarder attentivement
image_context: A person looking through binoculars at scenery
pronunciation_tips: The 'e' is schwa (ə). The 'ar' is like in 'car'. The 'de' rhymes with 'say'.
common_mistakes: Regular -er verb. It's 'je regarde', not 'je regardes'.

usage_notes: Irregular -re verb. Forms: prends, prend, prenons, prenez, prennent.
common_collocations: prendre le bus, prendre un café, prendre un médicament
image_context: A hand reaching out to take a coffee cup
pronunciation_tips: The 'en' is nasal. The 'dr' is guttural. The 'e' is like 'en' in 'bed'.
common_mistakes: Irregular verb! Forms include: prends, prend, prenons, prenez, prennent.

primary_meaning: to place or put something somewhere
usage_notes: Irregular -re verb. Forms: mets, met, mettons, mettez, mettent.
common_collocations: mettre la table, mettre un vêtement, mettre en place
image_context: A person placing an object on a shelf
example_1: Je mets le livre sur la table.
example_1_translation: I put the book on the table.
pronunciation_tips: The 'et' is like 'ɛt'. The 'tt' is a single sound. The 'r' is guttural.
common_mistakes: Double 't' in conjugation: 'mettons', 'mettez', 'mettent'.

primary_meaning: to transfer possession of something to another
usage_notes: Regular -er verb. Forms: donne, donnes, donnons, donnez, donnent.
common_collocations: donner un cadeau, donner des cours, donner une chance
image_search_query: two hands exchanging gift wrapped paper
image_context: Two hands exchanging a gift wrapped in paper
pronunciation_tips: The 'on' is nasal. The 'n' before 'e' is pronounced. Final 'e' is silent.
common_mistakes: Regular -er verb. Double 'n' in forms like 'donnons'.

usage_notes: Regular -er verb. Forms: montre, montres, montrons, montrez, montrent.
common_collocations: montrer des photos, montrer le chemin, montrer l'exemple
image_context: A person pointing at a map while explaining
pronunciation_tips: The 'on' is nasal. The 'tr' is soft. The final 'e' is silent.
common_mistakes: Regular -er verb. Don't confuse with 'monter' (to go up).

usage_notes: Regular -er verb. Forms: aide, aides, aidons, aidez, aident.
common_collocations: aider quelqu'un, aider à, avoir besoin d'aide
image_search_query: one person helping another reach something shelf
image_context: One person helping another reach something on a shelf
pronunciation_tips: The 'ai' is like 'ɛ'. The 'de' rhymes with 'say'.
common_mistakes: Regular -er verb. Don't say 'aidé' for present tense.
